title: Picometer control of a levitating milligram gravity sensor
authors: Dennis G. Uitenbroek, Jurriaan Langendorff, Tjerk H. Oosterkamp
abstract: Due to their exceptional isolation from the environment, magnetically levitated particles are explored as extremely sensitive mechanical sensors. For future gravity experiments on quantum superpositions, such systems need to be cooled close to their ground state. To demonstrate the combination of state of the art vibration isolation, milligram levitated high Q mechanical resonators and position detection with low noise, we present linear feedback cooling of a magnetically levitated gravity sensor to below 2 picometer amplitude and below 10 millikelvin mode temperature for two translational modes (the x- and y-mode) simultaneously. The sensor is a levitating permanent magnet in a type I superconducting trap, where its six resonance frequencies are measured with a superconducting coil coupled to a DC SQUID. This signal is measured with a lock-in amplifier and a feedback signal is sent to a piezoelectric actuator, allowing the cooling of resonant modes at 50.6 and 68.0 Hz simultaneously. These two translational modes have Q factors of $3.8 \cdot 10^6$ and $5.5 \cdot 10^6$ respectively. The experiment is mounted inside a dry dilution refrigerator where it is vibrationally attenuated with 110-130 dB at these frequencies. In this work, we discuss future improvements on the setup which may enable quantum ground state cooling on a magnetically levitated particle, that has previously been shown to be a gravitational sensor.
summary: Researchers have achieved picometer-scale control and millikelvin-scale cooling of a milligram-sized magnet levitated in a superconducting trap. By using active feedback cooling, they successfully reduced the thermal motion of two simultaneous mechanical modes. This setup provides a high-precision platform for future experiments testing the limits of quantum mechanics in the macroscopic regime.
main_problem: The challenge of cooling a macroscopic, milligram-scale mechanical resonator to its quantum ground state to enable future gravity experiments involving quantum superpositions.
main_result: Demonstrated simultaneous linear feedback cooling of two translational modes of a levitated magnet to below 2 picometer amplitude and below 10 millikelvin mode temperature.
method: Active damping via linear feedback cooling using a SQUID-based detection system, a lock-in amplifier, and a piezoelectric actuator to apply a phase-shifted force.
model_or_system: A 0.35 mg permanent magnet levitated in a type I superconducting tantalum trap via the Meissner effect, housed in a dry dilution refrigerator with multi-stage vibration isolation.
key_observables: Power Spectral Density (PSD), Amplitude Spectral Density (ASD), RMS amplitude, mode temperature, and phonon number.
important_parameters: Mass scale of ~0.35 mg, resonant frequencies of 50.6 Hz and 68.0 Hz, Q factors up to 5.5 x 10^6, and vibration attenuation of 110-130 dB.
main_assumptions: Assumes small displacements where magnetic flux change is linear; assumes the resonator behaves as a driven harmonic oscillator.
figures_summary: Schematics of the experimental setup and magnet composition; images of the superconducting trap and vibration isolation; plots of resonant modes (ASD) and the effect of feedback gain on mode temperature and amplitude.
paper_structure: Introduction to the scientific problem; description of the experimental platform and levitation setup; details on detection and calibration methods; presentation of cooling results for specific modes; discussion of noise upconversion and future paths to ground-state cooling.
why_interesting: This work demonstrates extreme precision control and cooling of a macroscopic object, which is a fundamental precursor to studying the interface between quantum mechanics and gravity and exploring decoherence in large-scale quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Overview of the experimental setup. (a) A schematic outline of the setup, the levitating permanent magnet is shown inside the superconducting trap. A pick-up coil attached to the lid of the trap picks up the varying flux of the moving levitated magnet and is detected using a two stage SQUID amplifier. We feedback cool the magnets motion by applying a phase shifted tone shaking the trap housing through a piezo and lock-in amplifier. (b) A close-up of the permanent magnet used in the experiment. It is composed of three 0.25 mm cubic neodymium iron boron parylene coated magnets stacked and glued together. A glass sphere with a diameter of 0.20 mm is attached to this stack of magnets...

FIG. 2: Typical amplitude spectral density (ASD) of the levitated magnet, the resonant modes of the magnet are indicated with the dotted lines. In this figure the 50 Hz European electrical noise is grayed out.

FIG. 4: Similar to Fig. 3, the linear feedback cooling on mode 4 is plotted. The different colored lines represent spectra taken with different linear gains. The legend shows the achieved mode temperatures with corresponding RMS amplitude and proportional gain values. Equal colors in this plot and in Fig. 3 indicate measurements are taken simultaneously.

FIG. 3: The linear feedback cooling on mode 3 is plotted. The different colored lines represent spectra taken with different linear gains. The legend shows the achieved mode temperatures with corresponding RMS amplitude and proportional gain values. Equal colors in this plot and in Fig. 4 indicate measurements are taken simultaneously.
